title-group.article-title: The regulatory significance of chondrocyte hypertrophy in maintaining chondrocyte homeostasis, regeneration and repair

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: Chondrocyte hypertrophy is a pivotal biological process in skeletal development and disease progression, with its precise regulation being essential for maintaining cartilage homeostasis and promoting tissue repair. This review systematically summarizes the molecular mechanisms of chondrocyte hypertrophy and its roles in both physiology and pathology, with a focus on its central involvement in osteoarthritis (OA), growth plate dysplasia, and heterotopic ossification. Key signaling pathways, including RUNX2, BMP, Wnt/β-catenin, and PTHrP, orchestrate hypertrophy through intricate crosstalk. Conversely, inhibitory factors such as SOX9, HIFs, and miRNAs preserve chondrocyte phenotype stability. Pathologically, dysregulated hypertrophy drives cartilage matrix degradation, metabolic reprogramming, and pro-inflammatory microenvironments, thereby exacerbating OA progression. Single-cell omics has unveiled cellular heterogeneity in OA cartilage, and innovative biomaterials combined with stem cell therapies offer promising regenerative approaches. However, limitations persist in understanding pathway interactions, replicating in vivo complexity in vitro, and translating findings to clinical applications. Future research should integrate multidisciplinary technologies to develop precise therapeutic strategies, advancing the treatment of cartilage-related disorders. This review is designed as a narrative review, aiming to systematically synthesize the molecular mechanisms, pathophysiological roles, and therapeutic implications of chondrocyte hypertrophy.

abstract.title: PLAIN LANGUAGE SUMMARY
abstract.p: Chondrocytes are the cells that make up cartilage, the smooth tissue that covers the ends of bones in joints. In healthy joints, these cells remain stable and help maintain cartilage. However, under certain conditions—such as injury, aging, or obesity—chondrocytes can undergo a process called “hypertrophy,” where they grow larger and change their behavior. While this process is normal during bone development and fracture healing, it becomes harmful when it happens in adult joint cartilage.

In osteoarthritis, a common joint disease, uncontrolled chondrocyte hypertrophy contributes to cartilage breakdown, pain, and joint dysfunction. This review explains how molecular signals inside cells control whether chondrocytes stay healthy or become hypertrophic. We discuss the key players—including proteins like RUNX2, SOX9, and signaling pathways such as BMP and Wnt—that act like switches to turn hypertrophy on or off.

Understanding these mechanisms opens new possibilities for treatment. Researchers are exploring ways to prevent unwanted hypertrophy using drugs, biomaterials, or stem cell therapies. However, challenges remain, including the need for better models to study these processes and safer treatments for patients.

list-item.p: Chondrocyte hypertrophy is a tightly regulated process essential for skeletal development but becomes pathological in osteoarthritis and other skeletal disorders.
RUNX2 serves as the central transcriptional executor of hypertrophy, integrating signals from BMP and Wnt pathways while being antagonized by SOX9 and PTHrP.
The BMP–Wnt–RUNX2 axis and the PTHrP–IHH feedback loop constitute the core regulatory hierarchy governing chondrocyte fate.
In osteoarthritis, hypertrophy is stage-dependent: early hypertrophy may reflect repair attempts, while late-stage hypertrophy drives matrix degradation and disease progression.
Single-cell transcriptomics has revealed distinct pre-hypertrophic chondrocyte populations that expand in OA cartilage.
Chondrocyte hypertrophy is linked to systemic metabolic and endocrine conditions, including diabetes and obesity.
Current regenerative therapies (ACI, MACI, iPSCs) face the challenge of preventing off-target hypertrophy; combination strategies with biomaterials and signaling modulators are under development.
Clinical translation requires better biomarkers for monitoring hypertrophy and rigorous evaluation of long-term safety and efficacy.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Chondrocyte hypertrophy is a pivotal biological process in skeletal development and disease progression. It is characterized by increased cell size, altered gene expression profiles, and extracellular matrix remodeling. This process plays an important role in normal bone development, but its aberrant regulation is also closely associated with a variety of skeletal disorders, such as osteoarthritis, epiphyseal dysplasia of cartilage growth plates and heterotopic ossification.

The current study shows that chondrocyte hypertrophy is regulated by a complex molecular network. Multiple key signaling pathways, including BMP, Wnt/β-catenin, TGF-β and PTHrP, are involved in chondrocyte hypertrophy regulation via sophisticated spatiotemporal control. Specifically, the transcription factor RUNX2, a major regulator of chondrocyte hypertrophy. It plays a central role in the hypertrophic process by modulating the expression of several key genes, including Col10a1, MMP13 and IHH.

In pathological states, aberrant regulation of chondrocyte hypertrophy can lead to serious clinical consequences. In osteoarthritis, for example, pathological hypertrophy leads to a decrease in the biomechanical properties of cartilage tissue, accompanied by changes in extracellular matrix components, such as a decrease in the expression of type II collagen and an up-regulation of collagen X expression. In addition, external stimuli such as inflammatory factors and mechanical stress can accelerate pathological hypertrophy of chondrocytes by affecting the activity of several signaling pathways.

Major challenges to chondrocyte regeneration and repair include limited tissue regeneration capacity and poor repair quality. Recent research has demonstrated that innovative biomaterials can promote regeneration of knee cartilage. This approach offers new hope for treatment. However, maintaining chondrocyte phenotypic stability and preventing hypertrophy remain key challenges.

In recent years, the understanding of chondrocyte hypertrophy has deepened with the application of new technologies such as single-cell transcriptomics. Significant cellular heterogeneity was found in OA cartilage tissue. Pre-inflammatory and pre-hypertrophic chondrocyte populations play key roles in disease progression. However, the interaction networks among multiple signaling pathways remain understudied, and current in vitro models cannot fully replicate the complexity of the in vivo microenvironment.

The review aims to systematically summarize the molecular mechanisms, pathophysiological effects, and clinical significance of chondrocyte hypertrophy. It focuses on the latest research advances and therapeutic strategies. By analyzing the limitations and knowledge gaps of existing studies, it provides guidance for future research directions and explores the potential for the development of novel therapeutic approaches, with a view to providing new ideas for the clinical treatment of related diseases.

sec.title: The process of chondrocyte differentiation and hypertrophy
The process of chondrocyte differentiation
sec.p: Chondrocyte differentiation is a highly complex and precisely regulated process involving the synergistic action of multiple key signaling pathways and transcription factors [1]. This process can be divided into four consecutive, well-characterized stages: recruitment and aggregation of mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs), chondrocyte precursor cell formation, chondrocyte maturation process, and terminal differentiation stage [2,3]. Figure 1 depicts the four sequential stages of chondrocyte differentiation: recruitment and aggregation of MSCs, chondrocyte precursor formation, chondrocyte maturation, and terminal differentiation.

fig.label: Figure 1.

caption.p: Chondrocyte differentiation can be divided into four distinct phases: recruitment and aggregation of MSCs, chondrocyte precursor cell formation, chondrocyte maturation, and the final differentiation phase. A. Recruitment and aggregation of MSCs is initiates chondrogenesis, with cells expressing early chondrogenic markers indicative of chondrocyte precursor formation. B. Chondrocyte precursors undergo rapid proliferation, forming a columnar arrangement in the growth plate and synthesizing large amounts of extracellular matrix. C. Subsequently, the cells enter the process of chondrocyte maturation, which is characterized by a significant increase in cell size and significant changes in gene expression profiles. D. Terminal differentiation is marked the entry of chondrocytes into the hypertrophic process, which is characterized by a significant increase in cell size, and hypertrophic chondrocytes ultimately undergo apoptosis or transdifferentiate into osteoblasts. (Abnormal activation of chondrocyte hypertrophy can lead to osteoarthritis).

fig.alt-text: Diagram illustrating mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) differentiation into chondrocytes, depicting cartilage homeostasis and osteoarthritis progression.
fig.long-desc: ** The diagram illustrates chondrogenesis stages: Panel A shows MSC recruitment and aggregation illustrated by a group of cells. Panel B depicts chondrocyte precursor formation in a purple matrix. Panel C highlights chondrocyte maturation in a structured layer. An arrow indicates the pathway towards cartilage homeostasis, represented by a healthy knee joint. A contrasting pathway illustrates chondrocyte hypertrophy leading to osteoarthritis, depicted as an inflamed joint with eroded cartilage. The entire figure emphasizes the sequential processes in cartilage development and health.

sec.p: According to recent studies, chondrocyte development begins with the differentiation of MSCs into the cartilage lineage under the regulation of SOX9 [4]. At this initial stage, cells begin to express early cartilage markers, such as collagen type II (COL2A1), signaling the formation of cartilage precursor cells [5,6]. Subsequently, these cells enter a period of rapid proliferation, forming a characteristic columnar arrangement in the growth plate while synthesizing large amounts of extracellular matrix components, including type II collagen and aggrecan (Aggrecan). Recruitment and aggregation of mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) is the initiating stage of chondrogenesis [7]. Recent studies have shown that MSCs have significant heterogeneity, and this heterogeneity has important implications for their differentiation potential and clinical applications. At this stage, transforming growth factor β (TGF-β) promotes the differentiation of MSCs toward the chondrocyte lineage by activating the Smad signaling pathway [8].

An important turning point in the developmental process is when some chondrocytes begin to enter a pre-hypertrophic state. At this stage, chondrocytes express Indian hedgehog (IHH) signaling molecules while retaining features of the proliferative phase [9]. The precursor cell formation phase of chondrocytes is mainly regulated by Sox9, which acts as a major transcription factor for chondrogenesis, forming a transcriptional complex with Sox5 and Sox6 that together regulate the expression of several chondrocyte-specific genes [10], including type II collagen (Col2a1) and aggrecan (Acan). Recent transcriptome analysis studies have revealed key regulatory networks at this stage, providing new insights into cartilage differentiation.

Subsequently, chondrocyte undergo maturation, characterized by a significant increase in cell size and changes in gene expression profiles, most notably the initiation of collagen type X (COL10A1) expression. This process is regulated by the PTHrP-IHH signaling pathway, which ensures that hypertrophy occurs at correct spatial and temporal scales [11]. During maturation, chondrocyte exhibit a rounded morphology and synthesize large amounts of extracellular matrix components. miRNAs are important regulators at this stage. For example, miR-218 promote early chondrogenesis while inhibiting later chondrocyte maturation [12]. This precise temporal and spatial regulation ensures proper chondrogenesis.

The terminal differentiation stage marks the entry of chondrocytes into the hypertrophic programme. This stage is characterized by a significant increase in cell size and the beginning of expression of collagen type X (Col X) and alkaline phosphatase [13]. As chondrocytes enter the hypertrophic phase, they produce high levels of alkaline phosphatase and extracellular matrix, and that the expression of these molecular markers is critical for the process of endochondral ossification [14].

Notably, chondrocyte hypertrophy varies among different cartilage types. In growth plate cartilage, hypertrophy is a normal developmental process necessary for longitudinal bone growth [15,16]. Hypertrophic chondrocytes eventually undergo apoptosis or transdifferentiation into osteoblasts [17], a process that is accompanied by matrix metalloproteinase 13 (MMP13) expression and calcification of the extracellular matrix [18]. In contrast, chondrocytes in articular cartilage normally maintain a stable non-hypertrophic state. However, in pathological conditions, articular cartilage may undergo abnormal hypertrophy, which is closely associated with the development of several osteoarticular diseases.

sec.title: Characteristics of chondrocyte hypertrophy
sec.p: Chondrocyte hypertrophy is a complex process involving significant changes in cell morphology, molecular expression, extracellular matrix composition and metabolic characteristics. Collectively, these changes characterize chondrocyte hypertrophy and are critical for understanding skeletal development and related diseases [15,19].
Morphologically, hypertrophic chondrocytes undergo significant enlargement. The morphological change is accompanied by reorganization and alterations in cell membrane properties [20,21].

Molecular markers are important tools for identifying and studying chondrocyte hypertrophy. Type X collagen, osteocalcin and IL6 have been shown to be important biomarkers of chondrocyte differentiation in hypertrophic chondrocytes, and the expression levels of these proteins increase significantly during hypertrophy [19]. Recent research has highlighted the role of non-coding RNAs in bone development and disease [22].

Extracellular matrix (ECM) remodeling is another key feature of chondrocyte hypertrophy. Recent studies have revealed mechanism of hypertrophy-induced matrix remodeling, and these changes ultimately lead to mineralization of articular cartilage [23]. Additionally, TMF inhibits chondrocyte hypertrophy by suppressing the BMPER/BMP4/Smad1 signaling pathway, thereby reducing collagen X and Runx2 expression [24].

Hypertrophic chondrocytes show significant metabolic pattern shifts. During osteoarthritis, chondrocytes undergo profound metabolic alterations, leading to impaired articular cartilage homeostasis [25]. This metabolic shift corresponds to an increase in cellular energy demand and also influences cell survival and fate decisions.

sec.title: Facilitating factors and their mechanisms
sec.p: Chondrocyte hypertrophy is a complex process that is precisely regulated by multiple signaling networks. Various facilitators act through specific signaling pathways and molecular mechanisms to promote the process of hypertrophy [19,26]. Understanding the mechanisms of these facilitators is important for the development of targeted therapeutic strategies.

The BMP signaling pathway plays a central regulatory role in chondrocyte hypertrophy. Recent studies reveal that BMPs directly regulate hypertrophy-associated genes and form complex regulatory network through interactions with other signaling pathways [27,28]. For example, BMP signaling influences chondrocyte fate by regulating RUNX2 expression and activity. In addition, TMF (Trichostatin M fraction) inhibited chondrocyte hypertrophy by inhibiting the BMPER/BMP4/Smad1 signaling pathway [24], which provides important clues for the development of new therapeutic strategies.

RUNX2, a major regulator of chondrocyte hypertrophy, regulates the hypertrophic process at multiple levels [29]. RUNX2 directly binds to promoter regions of genes such as Col10a1, MMP13, and IHH to regulate their expression [29,30]. Recent epigenetic studies have revealed that the transcriptional activity of RUNX2 is precisely regulated by histone modifications, and that this regulation is essential for maintaining normal cartilage development [31,32]. Furthermore, RUNX2 interacts with other transcription factors to regulate gene expression, forming a complex transcriptional network [30].

The TGF-β signaling pathway exhibits complex regulation during chondrocyte hypertrophy. TGF-β regulates chondrocyte fate through Smad-dependent and non-dependent pathways [33,34]. In the early stages, TGF-β promotes chondrocytes formation and maintains homeostasis by driving the differentiation of MSCs. In later stages, it may promote hypertrophy. This dual role is closely related to the spatiotemporal specificity of chondrogenesis [33,35–37]. Recent studies have found that the activity of the TGF-β signaling pathway is closely related to the metabolic profile and energy balance of chondrocytes, which provides new insights into understanding chondrocyte fate decisions [38].

The Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway is another important facilitative regulatory mechanism [39]. This pathway promotes chondrocyte proliferation and hypertrophy by regulating multiple downstream target genes, including RUNX2 and col10a1 [40]. Nuclear translocation of β-catenin, a key event in the initiation, is precisely controlled by multiple upstream regulators. Recent studies have highlighted the synergy between the Wnt signaling pathway and other facilitators, and in particular the interaction with the IHH signaling pathway plays an important role in regulating chondrocyte fate decisions [41,42].

Other promoters, such as IL-1β and VEGF, regulate chondrocyte hypertrophy through specific mechanisms. IL-1β promotes inflammation and matrix degradation primarily by activating the NF-κB signaling pathway [43]. VEGF influences chondrocyte fate by promoting angiogenesis and regulating extracellular matrix remodeling [44,45]. These factors form a complex interaction network that regulate the process of chondrocyte hypertrophy.

Critical gaps remain in understanding the temporal hierarchy of these facilitating factors. While BMP and Wnt signaling are widely accepted as pro-hypertrophic, their relative contribution at distinct stages of chondrocyte differentiation remains unclear. Moreover, most studies rely on overexpression systems or in vitro models that may not recapitulate the dynamic in vivo microenvironment. Future studies using conditional knockout models and spatiotemporal profiling are needed to establish causal relationships.

sec.title: Inhibitors and their mechanisms
sec.p: Inhibitory mechanisms of chondrocyte hypertrophy are essential for maintaining articular cartilage homeostasis. Multiple inhibitory factors act synergistically through different signaling pathways to regulate chondrocyte differentiation and fate decisions [46].

SOX9, a major chondrogenic transcription factor, centrally inhibits chondrocyte hypertrophy [4]. Recent studies have found that the Serinc5 protein can regulate the differentiation process of chondrocytes by inhibiting the function of SOX9, and this regulation is essential for maintaining the normal phenotype of chondrocytes [47,48]. Furthermore, the NF-κB-SOX9 pathway inhibits late stages of chondrocyte hypertrophy, providing new insights into chondrocyte fate decisions [49].

The PTHrP pathway is another key inhibitor. PTHrP dynamically regulates the chondrocyte hypertrophy by binding its receptor PTH1R and forming a negative feedback loop. IRE1α regulates the PTHrP-IHH feedback loop, thereby coordinating chondrocyte differentiation. In vitro, ERN1 deficiency accelerates cartilage hypertrophy and mineralization by disrupting IHH-PTHrP feedback homeostasis [11].

Hypoxia-inducible factors (HIFs) play a key role in the hypoxic microenvironment of chondrogenesis. HIF-1α maintains energy homeostasis in chondrocytes by regulating glycolysis and mitochondrial function [50,51]. Under hypoxic conditions, the stability of HIF-1α increases, which in turn activates the expression of downstream genes, including VEGF and glycolysis-related enzymes. This regulation is critical for chondrocyte survival and differentiation [52]. A recent study also found that HIFs influence chondrocyte fate by regulating autophagy and mitochondrial function [53].

Other important inhibitors include multiple molecules and signaling pathways. For example, miR-140-5p regulates the hypertrophic process of chondrocytes by targeting Sox9 and Adamts5 [54,55]. Furthermore, recent studies suggest that specific miRNAs in exosomes may also be involved in the inhibition of chondrocyte hypertrophy [5].

Despite the well-established inhibitory roles of SOX9 and PTHrP, the interplay between these pathways and pro-hypertrophic signals remains incompletely understood [56]. For instance, how SOX9 suppresses RUNX2 activity under physiological conditions, and how this suppression is lost in osteoarthritis, requires further mechanistic elucidation. Additionally, the cell-type specificity of these inhibitors across different cartilage types (articular vs. growth plate) warrants deeper investigation.

sec.title: Integration and balance of regulatory networks
sec.p: Chondrocyte hypertrophy is regulated by complex signaling networks that maintain cartilage homeostasis through sophisticated integration and balancing mechanisms. Recent studies indicate that synergistic interactions among multiple signaling pathways are critical for the regulating chondrocyte fate [19,26,57].

The regulatory network depicted in Figure 2 illustrates the integrated control of chondrocyte hypertrophy. At the core, RUNX2 serves as the central hub, integrating pro-hypertrophic signals from BMP and Wnt pathways while being suppressed by SOX9 and PTHrP. The BMP–Wnt–RUNX2 axis forms the primary pro-hypertrophic cascade, whereas SOX9 and PTHrP act as counterbalancing forces. The IHH–PTHrP feedback loop introduces a temporal dimension, ensuring that hypertrophy occurs in a controlled, sequential manner. Disruption of this balanced network—whether by genetic mutations, inflammatory stimuli, or mechanical overload—leads to pathological hypertrophy characteristic of osteoarthritis and other skeletal disorders.

fig.label: Figure 2.

caption.p: Regulatory network of chondrocyte hypertrophy. Chondrocyte hypertrophy is governed by an integrated signaling network with hierarchical organization. Pro-hypertrophic factors (BMP, Wnt/β-catenin, RUNX2) are depicted in red, while anti-hypertrophic factors (SOX9, PTHrP, HIFs) are shown in blue. Solid arrows indicate direct activation; dashed arrows indicate indirect or multi-step regulation; blunt-ended lines indicate inhibition. Key nodes: BMP and Wnt converge on RUNX2, which drives expression of hypertrophy markers (COL10A1, MMP13). PTHrP forms a negative feedback loop with IHH to delay hypertrophy. SOX9 antagonizes RUNX2 and maintains chondrocyte phenotype. HIF-1α acts as a contextual inhibitor, particularly under hypoxic conditions. The balance between these opposing forces determines chondrocyte fate.

fig.alt-text: Diagram of signaling pathways involving TGF-ß, BMP, WNT, TLR, and their roles in chondrocyte hypertrophy.
fig.long-desc: ** This diagram illustrates the signaling pathways influencing chondrocyte hypertrophy, showing TGF-ß, BMP, WNT, TLR, and hypoxia. Pathways include TGF-ß activating RAS, leading to Smad phosphorylation, BMP activating Smad1/5/8 and RUNX2, and WNT signaling through ß-Catenin. TLR activates inflammation pathways. Hypoxia influences HIF1-a/ß. The outcomes include changes in markers like Aggrecan, COL2A1, and MMPs, linked to hypertrophy.

sec.p: Signaling pathways interactions constitute the core of the regulatory network. Mapping the enhancer landscape during the prehypertrophic phase of cartilage reveals the synergistic action of multiple signaling pathways, which control chondrocyte fate decisions by regulating specific transcription factor networks [26]. During this process, the expression levels of two key transcription factors, SOX9 and RUNX2, are tightly regulated, and the balance between them is essential for maintaining the normal phenotype of chondrocytes [58,59].

Feedback regulatory mechanisms critically maintain cartilage homeostasis. Recent studies have revealed that IRE1α coordinates chondrocyte growth and differentiation by regulating the PTHrP-IHH feedback loop. ERN1 deficiency accelerates chondrocyte hypertrophy and cartilage mineralization by disrupting the homeostasis of the IHH and the PTHrP/PTH1R feedback loops. This feedback regulation not only affects chondrocyte differentiation, but also closely related to the endoplasmic reticulum stress response [11].

Microenvironmental factors significantly influence chondrocyte fate decisions. Cartilage tissue-specific transcriptional features reflect cell type-specific regulatory mechanisms closely linked to the tissue microenvironment [60]. In addition, external stimuli, such as mechanical stress and inflammatory factors, regulate of chondrocyte fate by modulating the activity of several signaling pathways [61,62].

sec.title: Hierarchical regulation and pathway crosstalk
sec.p: The regulatory network governing chondrocyte hypertrophy is not merely a collection of parallel pathways but exhibits a clear hierarchical structure. At the apex of this hierarchy, RUNX2 functions as the central transcriptional executor of hypertrophy, directly activating downstream targets including Col10a1, Mmp13, and Ihh [29,40]. RUNX2 activity is, in turn, modulated by upstream signaling cascades.

BMP signaling acts as a master upstream regulator, promoting RUNX2 expression and transcriptional activity through SMAD-dependent mechanisms. Wnt/β-catenin signaling converges on RUNX2 by facilitating its nuclear translocation and transcriptional co-activation [63,64]. Importantly, these pathways exhibit synergistic crosstalk: BMP and Wnt often co-operate to drive hypertrophy, yet their relative contributions may vary across developmental stages and pathological contexts.

The SOX9–RUNX2 antagonism represents a critical node in fate determination. Under homeostatic conditions [65], SOX9 suppresses RUNX2 expression and activity, maintaining the articular chondrocyte phenotype [49,66]. Upon hypertrophic induction, RUNX2 expression rises while SOX9 is downregulated. This reciprocal relationship is tightly controlled by upstream signals: PTHrP, for instance, sustains SOX9 expression and inhibits RUNX2 via the PTH1R–CREB axis [67].

Integrated feedback loops further refine this hierarchy. The IHH–PTHrP loop provides spatial and temporal control: IHH produced by pre-hypertrophic chondrocytes stimulates PTHrP expression, which in turn delays hypertrophy [68]. Disruption of this loop—as seen in growth plate dysplasias or osteoarthritis—leads to premature or excessive hypertrophy. Understanding these hierarchical and reciprocal interactions is essential for developing targeted interventions that modulate hypertrophy without disrupting cartilage homeostasis.

sec.title: Physiological significance of chondrocyte hypertrophy
sec.p: Chondrocyte hypertrophy is a key event in skeletal development and tissue repair, and its phenotypic changes and functional transitions are important for maintaining the normal development and function of the skeletal system. This complex biological process involves cell volume increase, altered gene expression profiles, and extracellular matrix remodeling, and participates in several important physiological processes through sophisticated spatiotemporal regulation [69,70].

sec.title: Role in chondrocyte formation and maintenance
sec.p: Chondrocyte hypertrophy exhibits dual properties during the formation and maintenance of cartilage tissue. Hypertrophied chondrocytes participate in the dynamic remodeling process of the cartilage matrix by regulating the expression and activity of matrix metalloproteinases [71]. These cells maintain cartilage homeostasis by secreting specific extracellular matrix proteins and growth factors that build and sustain the tissue microenvironment [72]. Notably, the degree of chondrocyte hypertrophy needs to be tightly controlled, and excessive hypertrophy may lead to degeneration and pathological changes in cartilage tissue. Multiple signaling pathways, including the BMP and Wnt interaction, regulate this balance [73].

sec.title: Role in fracture healing process
sec.p: During the early inflammatory response phase after a fracture occurs, a hematoma forms at the site of trauma and large amounts of inflammatory and growth factors are released. These molecular signals recruit MSCs, inflammatory cells and vascular endothelial cells to reach the site of injury [74,75]. The intensity and duration of the early inflammatory response was found to have a significant impact on the choice of subsequent healing modalities [76].

During endochondral ossification, MSCs recruited to the fracture site initially differentiate into chondrocytes, forming a cartilage template [77]. These chondrocytes proliferate rapidly and secrete type II collagen-rich extracellular matrix, which forms the central region of the bone scab [78]. Subsequently, chondrocytes undergo gradual hypertrophy, express type X collagen, and eventually undergo programmed cell death, creating conditions for neovascularization and osteoblast invasion [79,80].

In the area near the fracture end, osteoblasts directly carry out the osteogenic process in a manner known as intramembranous ossification. Osteoblasts secrete a type I collagen-rich bone matrix and participate in the mineralization of the matrix [81]. This direct osteogenesis mainly occurs in regions with adequate blood supply and mechanically stability [82].

The choice between the two modes of bone formation is influenced by several factors. Mechanical stability is a critical factor. Direct osteogenesis occurs in stable environments, while endochondral ossification predominates in areas with greater micromotion [82–84].

Figure 3 depict the two modes of bone formation during fracture healing: intramembranous and endochondral ossification. Chondrocyte hypertrophy is a central component of endochondral ossification during fracture healing. By secreting specific growth factors and cytokines, hypertrophied chondrocytes promote angiogenesis and bone tissue formation. This process begins with the formation of a cartilage scab and is followed by a complex process of cellular differentiation and matrix remodeling that culminates in the reconstruction of bone tissue [13,80]. Hypertrophied chondrocytes secrete factors such as VEGF to promote neovascularization and facilitate osteoblast migration and differentiation [45]. This synergistic effect has an important impact on the quality and speed of fracture healing.

fig.label: Figure 3.

caption.p: The process of fracture healing (cited in Section 4.2). There are two main modes: intramembranous ossification and endochondral ossification. During the early inflammatory phase, TGF-β and BMP can recruit MSCs to the fracture hematoma. In endochondral ossification, recruited MSCs first differentiate into chondrocytes, forming a cartilage template. These chondrocytes proliferate rapidly and secrete a type II collagen-rich extracellular matrix, forming the central bone callus. Chondrocytes then undergo hypertrophy, express type X collagen, and ultimately experience apoptosis, enabling neovascularization and osteoblast invasion. Meanwhile, near the fracture ends, osteoblasts directly form bone through intramembranous ossification. Osteoblasts secrete a type I collagen-rich bone matrix and promote its mineralization.

fig.alt-text: Flowchart depicting mesenchymal stem cell differentiation pathways into chondrocytes and osteoblasts, leading to bone formation.
fig.long-desc: ** The diagram illustrates the differentiation of mesenchymal stem cells into chondrocytes and osteoblasts, denoting key markers like type II collagen, aggrecan, and osteocalcin. Chondrocytes undergo processes such as hypertrophic differentiation and apoptosis, culminating in endochondral ossification. Meanwhile, osteoblasts and osteoclasts, with a matrix of type I collagen, lead to intramembranous ossification and capillary ingrowth. Arrows connect these pathways, highlighting their roles in bone formation.

sec.title: Role in skeletal development
sec.p: During skeletal development, chondrocyte hypertrophy plays a decisive role in growth plate development and bone length growth. Growth plate chondrocytes undergo tightly regulated differentiation, including proliferation, pre-hypertrophy, and terminal hypertrophy [4]. This process is precisely controlled by several transcription factors, with dynamic changes in the expression levels of SOX9 and RUNX2 being particularly critical. Hypertrophied chondrocytes secrete extracellular matrix proteins that support the microenvironment necessary for subsequent ossification. Additionally, these cells influence the behavior of surrounding cells via autocrine and paracrine mechanisms, thereby coordinating skeletal development [85,86].

sec.title: Development of osteoarthritis
sec.p: Pathological chondrocyte hypertrophy in osteoarthritis (OA) is associated with aberrant activation of RUNX2. Single-cell transcriptome analyses revealed that pre-hypertrophic phenotype chondrocytes were significantly enriched in OA cartilage tissues and their transcriptional profiles were closely associated with aberrant activation of RUNX2 [87]. RUNX2 overexpression in OA chondrocytes not only promotes hypertrophy through direct up-regulation of COL10A1 and MMP13 [18,88], but also enhances its transcriptional activity through epigenetic modifications, such as increased H3K4me3 in the RUNX2 promoter region [89]. This aberrant activation forms a positive feedback loop with mechanical stress-induced calcium inward flow (via Piezo1 channels) and ROS accumulation, further exacerbating the hypertrophy process [90,91]. Mechanical stress is key trigger of chondrocyte hypertrophy [92]. Abnormal mechanical loading was found to promote the transformation of chondrocytes to a hypertrophic phenotype by activating mechanosensitive ion channels and integrin signaling pathways, leading to calcium homeostasis imbalance and cytoskeletal remodeling [93,94]. Temporal dynamics of chondrocyte hypertrophy in OA progression. The role of hypertrophy varies across disease stages. In early OA, chondrocytes exhibit a partial hypertrophic phenotype characterized by increased COL10A1 and MMP13 expression [37], which may represent an attempted repair response [36]. However, as disease progresses to moderate and advanced stages, hypertrophy becomes pathologically amplified, driven by sustained mechanical stress, inflammatory cytokines, and metabolic dysregulation. Single-cell transcriptomic studies have identified pre-hypertrophic chondrocyte clusters that expand markedly in OA cartilage, correlating with matrix degradation and disease severity [95]. This transition from a controlled to a dysregulated hypertrophic program represents a critical juncture in OA pathogenesis, suggesting that interventions targeting hypertrophy may be most effective if applied early.

Metabolic reprogramming is a hallmark of chondrocyte hypertrophy. OA chondrocytes exhibit significant metabolic alterations, including enhanced glycolysis, mitochondrial dysfunction, and disrupted lipid metabolism [96,97]. These alterations accelerate chondrocyte hypertrophy by disrupting energy metabolism and increasing oxidative stress levels [98].

Pathologically, hypertrophied chondrocytes show characteristic morphological and functional changes. Significant cell volume increase is accompanied by changes in extracellular matrix components, such as decreased expression of type II collagen and upregulation of type X collagen expression [99]. This phenotypic transformation leads to a decrease in the biomechanical properties of cartilage tissue.

The inflammatory microenvironment synergistically promotes chondrocyte hypertrophy. The high expression of IL-6 and TNF-α in OA synovium not only induces the expression of MMPs through the NF-κB signaling pathway [100], but also promotes the nuclear translocation of RUNX2 through activation of the JAK-STAT3 pathway [101]. The inflammatory microenvironment plays a facilitating role in chondrocyte hypertrophy. pro-inflammatory factors such as IL-1β and TNF-α induce matrix metalloproteinase expression and accelerate extracellular matrix degradation through activation of the NF-κB and MAPK signaling pathways [102]. Meanwhile, these inflammatory factors also promote the production of more pro-inflammatory mediators by chondrocytes, forming a vicious circle.

Crosstalk between chondrocyte hypertrophy and fibrosis. Chondrocyte hypertrophy and tissue fibrosis are closely linked pathological processes in OA [103]. Hypertrophic chondrocytes secrete pro-fibrotic factors including TGF-β, CTGF, and periostin, which can promote synovial fibrosis and subchondral bone sclerosis. Conversely, fibrotic changes in the joint capsule and synovium alter biomechanical loading, further promoting chondrocyte hypertrophy [104]. This bidirectional relationship is mediated by shared signaling pathways, particularly TGF-β/Smad and Wnt/β-catenin [105], which regulate both chondrocyte phenotype and fibroblast activation [106,107]. Targeting these shared pathways may offer dual benefits in mitigating both hypertrophic and fibrotic components of OA pathology.

sec.title: Other bone-related diseases
sec.p: Beyond osteoarthritis, chondrocyte hypertrophy contributes to the development of several skeletal diseases.

sec.title: Osteosclerosis is a pathological condition defined by abnormal bone density increase
sec.p: Studies show that abnormal chondrocytes hypertrophy is involved in the development of osteosclerosis by disrupting bone remodeling [108]. At the molecular level, hypertrophic chondrocytes secrete large amounts of bone BMPs and other growth factors that over-activate osteoblasts, leading to a dysfunctional balance between bone formation and bone resorption [86,109]. Recent studies have found that aberrant activation of the mTOR signaling pathway plays a key role in this process. Over-activated mTOR signaling promotes the transformation of chondrocytes toward a hypertrophic phenotype [110,111], while upregulating the expression of osteogenesis-related transcription factors such as RUNX2 and Osterix. This metabolic disturbance ultimately leads to the development of bone over deposition and osteosclerosis [112].

sec.title: Epiphyseal dysplasia of cartilage growth plates is an inherited disorder affecting bone growth and development
sec.p: Clinically, these disorders usually present as generalized skeletal development [70]. Patients often exhibit marked growth retardation, disproportionately short limbs, and characteristic skeletal deformities [15]. Recent clinical studies indicate that, in addition to skeletal abnormalities, patients may develop systemic complications due to the essential roles of cartilage in multiple organs. Abnormal regulation of chondrocyte hypertrophy is a key pathogenic mechanism [113]. During normal development, chondrocyte proliferation, differentiation and hypertrophy require strict spatial and temporal regulation. However, in this disease, genetic mutations disrupt regulatory mechanisms, leading to a complex relationship between defective chondrocyte hypertrophy and metaphyseal dysplasia [114]. Abnormalities chondrocyte hypertrophy can manifest as excessive hypertrophy, hypertrophy defects, or timing disturbances, each resulting in distinct disease phenotypes.

Regarding disorders associated with excessive hypertrophy, FGFR3 mutations can lead to chondrodysplasia (Achondroplasia). This mutation activates the FGFR3 pathway, causing chondrocytes to enter the hypertrophic stage prematurely [115]. Aberrant FGFR3 signaling affects chondrocyte proliferation and alters extracellular matrix synthesis through the MAPK pathway, ultimately leading to structural disruption of the growth plate and limitation of bone growth [108].

Insufficient hypertrophy can cause metaphyseal chondrodysplasia, Schmid type. This disorder is strongly associated with mutations in the COL10A1 gene, which encodes type X collagen, a characteristic marker of hypertrophic chondrocytes [116]. COL10A1 mutations prevent normal chondrocytes hypertrophy, leading to structural abnormalities in the growth plate and affecting the process of endochondral ossification, which ultimately manifests as skeletal dysplasia [70].

Disturbances in the PTHrP-IHH signaling pathway can lead to multiple epiphyseal dysplasia (MED). This pathway is essential for the spatiotemporal regulation of chondrocyte hypertrophy [70]. Recent studies have demonstrate that abnormal PTHrP or IHH disrupts the balance between chondrocyte proliferation and hypertrophy, leading to disorganization of the growth plate. This imbalance manifeste as premature hypertrophy in some areas and delayed hypertrophy in others, ultimately causing uneven epiphyseal development [71].

Pseudoachondroplasia caused by mutations in the COMP gene provides another important mechanistic paradigm [117]. COMP protein is essential for maintaining the integrity of the extracellular matrix of cartilage. When COMP is mutated, the abnormal protein accumulates in chondrocytes, triggering an endoplasmic reticulum stress response that affects chondrocyte survival and differentiation. This stress response interferes with the normal process of hypertrophy, leading to structural disruption of the growth plate [55,118].

Molecular genetic studies indicate that mutations in several key genes are associated with the disease, including COMP, MATN3 and COL9A1/2/3 [119]. Mutations in these genes disrupt chondrocyte hypertrophy, impairing normal development of the growth plate. In particular, mutations in the COMP gene lead to protein misfolding and endoplasmic reticulum stress, which in turn affects chondrocyte survival and function [118,120].

sec.title: Heterotopic ossification: pathological bone formation in non-skeletal tissue
sec.p: Abnormal chondrocyte hypertrophy significantly contributes to this process. Under stimuli such as trauma and inflammation, local MSCs are activated and differentiate into chondrocytes, which subsequently undergo pathological hypertrophy and eventually lead to heterotopic ossification [121,122]. Studies at the molecular level have shown that aberrant activation of the BMP signaling pathway is a key factor in the induction of heterotopic ossification. In particular, overexpression of BMP2 promotes abnormal differentiation and hypertrophy of chondrocytes [123]. In addition, elevated inflammatory factors such as TNF-α and IL-6 may also promote chondrocyte hypertrophy and heterotopic ossification by activating the NF-κB signaling pathway [124]. Recent studies show that exosomes are involved in heterotopic ossification [125]. Exosomes from hypertrophic chondrocytes contain specific miRNAs and growth factors that influence ossification of surrounding tissues via paracrine mechanisms [126].

sec.title: Chondrocyte hypertrophy in systemic metabolic and endocrine disorders
sec.p: Emerging evidence links chondrocyte hypertrophy to systemic metabolic and endocrine conditions. Diabetes mellitus is associated with accelerated OA progression, and hyperglycemia has been shown to promote chondrocyte hypertrophy through advanced glycation end-products and oxidative stress [127,128]. Obesity contributes not only through mechanical overload but also via adipokine secretion; leptin, for instance, can potentiate hypertrophic differentiation while adiponectin may exert protective effects [129,130].

Parathyroid hormone (PTH) and PTHrP signaling—beyond its well-established role in growth plate regulation—may mediate cartilage-bone crosstalk in metabolic bone diseases [131]. Hyperparathyroidism has been implicated in articular cartilage calcification and hypertrophy-like changes. Additionally, thyroid hormones influence chondrocyte differentiation, with hypothyroidism delaying and hyperthyroidism potentially accelerating hypertrophy [132].

Understanding these systemic influences is crucial, as they suggest that management of chondrocyte hypertrophy in OA may require not only local joint-targeted strategies but also systemic metabolic control [133]. The interplay between endocrine factors and local signaling pathways represents an important frontier for future investigation.

sec.title: Challenges in chondrocyte regeneration and repair
sec.p: Systematic analyses of current studies reveal that chondrocyte regeneration and repair face multiple challenges. The primary problem is the specificity of cartilage tissue: absence of vascular distribution, limited self-repair capacity, and a complex extracellular matrix [134]. These characteristics limit the effectiveness of conventional treatments. Although the roles of multiple signaling pathways have been elucidated in depth, the interaction network between multiple pathways has been relatively understudied. Specifically, the synergistic mechanism of BMP, Wnt/β-catenin and PTHrP pathways during chondrocyte hypertrophy remain unclear [11,63]. Existing in vitro model systems struggle to fully simulate the complexity of the in vivo microenvironment, which affects the clinical relevance of the findings [135]. In particular, there is a lack of experimental models that accurately reflect the in vivo environment when studying the regulatory mechanisms of chondrocyte hypertrophy.

sec.title: Importance of regulating chondrocyte hypertrophy
sec.p: Regulation of chondrocyte hypertrophy is critical for cartilage regeneration and repair. Evidence indicates that moderate hypertrophy is necessary for cartilage development and repair [136], whereas excessive hypertrophy leads to pathological changes [137]. Recent studies have found that precise control of chondrocyte hypertrophy can be achieved by regulating key signaling pathways. For example, balanced expression of SOX9 and RUNX2 is essential for maintaining a normal chondrocyte phenotype [29,49]. This provides a theoretical basis for the developing novel therapeutic strategies.

sec.title: Clinical significance and outlook
sec.p: Clinically, novel therapeutic approaches such as stem cell therapy and gene therapy show promising prospects. In particular, iPSC technology provides new possibilities for individualized therapies. However, these therapeutic approaches still face many challenges during clinical translation, such as the durability of therapeutic effects, safety assessment, and cost-effectiveness. Emerging technologies create new research opportunities. The use of application intelligence in data analysis and drug screening has significantly enhanced research efficiency. Organ-on-a-chip and organoid technologies provides better in vitro model systems for research. Future research directions may focus on: strengthening multidisciplinary cross-research and integrating the strengths of biology, materials science and computational science; developing more accurate in vitro modeling systems to improve the reliability of research results; and strengthening clinical translational research, especially the assessment of long-term safety and efficacy.

sec.title: Clinical trials and translational perspectives
sec.p: Despite the strong mechanistic rationale for targeting chondrocyte hypertrophy, clinical translation remains in early stages. Several approaches are under investigation: Wnt/β-catenin pathway inhibitors, such as lorecivivint (SM04690), have entered clinical trials for knee OA [138]. While primarily investigated for disease-modifying effects, these agents may indirectly suppress chondrocyte hypertrophy. Phase II trials have shown preliminary evidence of structural modification and symptom improvement. BMP signaling modulators are being explored, though their dual roles in chondrogenesis and hypertrophy necessitate careful titration [139]. PTHrP analogs (e.g., teriparatide) have been evaluated in OA [140], with mixed results; their effects on hypertrophy may depend on dosing and disease stage. Cell-based therapies, including autologous chondrocyte implantation (ACI) and matrix-induced ACI (MACI), face the challenge of maintaining chondrocyte phenotype and preventing hypertrophy post-implantation [141,142]. Emerging strategies incorporate gene editing (e.g., CRISPR-mediated SOX9 upregulation or RUNX2 suppression) to enhance phenotypic stability. Stem cell therapies (mesenchymal stem cells, induced pluripotent stem cells) offer regenerative potential but carry risks of off-target hypertrophy and ectopic ossification [65,143]. Ongoing trials are evaluating optimized differentiation protocols and scaffold-based delivery to mitigate these risks. Table 1 Key regulators of chondrocyte hypertrophy

caption.p: Key regulators of chondrocyte hypertrophy (cited in Section 6.4).

tr.td: Central executor; promotes COL10A1, MMP13, IHH
Activated by BMP, Wnt; inhibited by SOX9, PTHrP

Synergizes with Wnt; antagonized by PTHrP

Forms negative feedback with IHH; maintains SOX9

sec.p: A key limitation of current clinical efforts is the lack of biomarkers to monitor hypertrophic activity non-invasively. Development of imaging or serum-based biomarkers could facilitate patient stratification and response monitoring in future trials [56].

sec.title: Chondrocyte hypertrophy in cartilage repair and regenerative therapies
sec.p: Successful cartilage repair requires not only the generation of new tissue but also the prevention of pathological hypertrophy. Autologous chondrocyte implantation (ACI) and its matrix-assisted variant (MACI) have been widely used for focal cartilage defects [141,142]; however, post-implantation hypertrophy and graft failure remain concerns. Strategies to mitigate hypertrophy in ACI/MACI include the use of phenotypically stable chondrocytes and scaffolds that mimic the native articular cartilage microenvironment.

Cell-free scaffolds functionalized with chondroinductive factors (e.g., TGF-β3, SOX9) offer the advantage of recruiting endogenous cells while avoiding the hypertrophic risks associated with ex vivo cell expansion [144,145]. Recent studies demonstrate that scaffolds incorporating Wnt inhibitors or BMP modulators can promote cartilage regeneration while suppressing hypertrophic differentiation.

Induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) represent a promising cell source for cartilage repair [146]. However, iPSC-derived chondrocytes exhibit a tendency toward hypertrophy, resembling fetal growth plate chondrocytes rather than stable articular chondrocytes. Advanced differentiation protocols incorporating PTHrP, HIF-1α stabilization, or mechanical loading are being developed to guide iPSC-derived cells toward an articular rather than hypertrophic phenotype [147].

Endogenous cell homing strategies leverage chemotactic signals (e.g., stromal cell-derived factor 1, SDF-1) to recruit resident mesenchymal progenitors to defect sites [148]. This approach may bypass the hypertrophic risks associated with in vitro expansion, though careful regulation of the recruited cells’ differentiation trajectory remains essential [149].

Across all regenerative approaches, the central challenge remains: achieving sufficient matrix production and defect filling while maintaining chondrocyte phenotypic stability and preventing terminal differentiation. Future advances will likely emerge from combination strategies integrating biomaterial design, controlled release of signaling molecules, and cell engineering.

sec.title: Limitations and future perspectives
sec.p: Despite substantial advances, several limitations constrain current understanding. First, the majority of mechanistic studies are derived from in vitro models that lack the complex biomechanical and inflammatory cues present in native cartilage. Second, the temporal dynamics of pathway crosstalk remain poorly characterized, with most studies capturing static endpoints. Third, heterogeneity among chondrocyte populations—recently uncovered by single-cell transcriptomics—has not been fully integrated into experimental models. Future research should prioritize: (1) development of physiologically relevant in vitro systems; (2) integration of multi-omics approaches to map pathway dynamics; (3) translation of mechanistic insights into targeted therapies with rigorous preclinical validation.
